1: וַיִּהְיוּ חַיֵּי שָׂרָה מֵאָה שָׁנָה וְעֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וְשֶׁבַע שָׁנִים — "The years of Sarah's life were one hundred years and twenty years and seven years." The unusual enumeration (one hundred + twenty + seven, rather than the standard "127 years") may emphasize each unit separately or reflect an archaic form of numerical notation. Sarah is the only woman in the OT whose age at death is explicitly recorded — the covenant wife's lifespan is narratively as significant as the patriarch's. The phrase ḥayyê śārāh (the life/years of Sarah) gives the chapter its traditional Jewish title (Parashat Ḥayyê Śārāh).
2: וַתָּמָת שָׂרָה בְּקִרְיַת אַרְבַּע הִוא חֶבְרוֹן — "Sarah died in Kiriath-arba, that is Hebron." Qiryat ʾarbaʿ (city of four / city of Arba) — the ancient name for Hebron; Josh 14:15 identifies Arba as "the greatest man among the Anakim" (a giant). The parenthetical "(that is, Hebron)" uses the later, better-known name for the reader. Hebron is where Abraham had his altar (13:18) and his Amorite allies (14:13, 24). לִסְפֹּד לְשָׂרָה וְלִבְכֹּתָהּ (to mourn and to weep for Sarah) — two verbs: sāpad (to strike the breast, wail formally in lamentation — a ritualized mourning gesture) and bākāh (to weep — the emotional tears). Both forms of mourning are given to Sarah.
3: מֵעַל פְּנֵי מֵתוֹ (mēʿal pĕnê mētô, from before/beside his dead) — "from beside his dead wife." The Hebrew literally is "from the face of his dead one" — pāneh (face) in proximity language; Abraham had been sitting with the body, then rises to conduct the business of burial. The posture of rising from beside the dead body marks the transition from grief to practical action. וַיְדַבֵּר אֶל-בְּנֵי-חֵת (he spoke to the sons of Heth) — the Hittites (ḥittîm) are the resident population of Hebron; Abraham must negotiate with them for burial rights.
4: גֵּר-וְתוֹשָׁב אָנֹכִי עִמָּכֶם (gēr-wĕtôšāḇ ʾānōḵî ʿimmāḵem) — "I am a sojourner and resident-alien among you." The double status: gēr (sojourner, resident alien without citizen rights, legal protection through hospitality conventions) + tôšāḇ (temporary settler, one who dwells temporarily in a place that belongs to others). The combined phrase appears in Lev 25:23 where God tells Israel that "the land is mine; for you are strangers and sojourners (gērîm wĕtôšāḇîm) with me." The self-description is Abraham's own — he is the covenant heir of the land and yet has no legal ownership of any of it; he must buy burial space from its current occupants. 1 Pet 2:11 echoes this self-identification for Christians in the world.
5: The Hittites' response (vv. 5-6) follows a formal protocol: they address Abraham with honorific recognition, concede his request in principle, and offer the most generous possible terms — all family tombs are open to him. The negotiation unfolds in the formal pattern of ANE public transaction: open offer → counter-request (Abraham specifies what he actually wants) → second negotiation round (vv. 7-16). The repetition of bowing (wayyišaḥû, v. 7) and formal address marks each stage of the legal protocol.
6: נְשִׂיא אֱלֹהִים אַתָּה בְּתוֹכֵנוּ (nĕśîʾ ʾĔlōhîm ʾattāh bĕtôḵēnû) — "you are a prince of God among us." Nāśîʾ (chieftain, prince, elevated one) — the highest social title the Hittites can give; ʾĔlōhîm as a superlative intensifier (a "divine/god-like prince") or literally "God's prince" — acknowledging that divine blessing rests on Abraham. The same recognition occurs with Abimelech (21:22: "God is with you in all you do"). The pagan recognition of God's blessing on Abraham is a recurring narrative pattern. בְּמִבְחַר קְבָרֵינוּ קְבֹר אֶת-מֵתֶךָ — "bury your dead in the choicest of our tombs" — the offer is maximally generous; Abraham will nevertheless insist on buying a specific site.
7: וַיָּקָם אַבְרָהָם וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ לְעַם-הָאָרֶץ — "Abraham rose and bowed down before the people of the land." Wayyiqqom wayyištaḥû — rising and bowing is the formal greeting gesture in ANE diplomatic and legal contexts; the bowing here acknowledges the Hittites' authority over their land and their willingness to negotiate. The politeness is part of the legal protocol — Abraham must be deferential to secure the sale on terms he desires.
8: אִם-יֶשׁ אֶת-נַפְשְׁכֶם לִקְבֹּר אֶת-מֵתִי מִלְּפָנַי — "if you are willing to bury my dead from before me." The Hebrew idiom im-yēš ʾet-napšĕḵem (if it is with your soul = if it is your will) treats the soul/will as the seat of desire and intention. Abraham frames his request as conditional on their genuine willingness — he is not demanding but soliciting consent. שִׁמְעוּנִי וּפִגְעוּ-לִי בְּעֶפְרוֹן — "hear me and intercede for me with Ephron" — pāgaʿ (to meet, encounter, fall upon; in the Hiphil: to cause to meet, to intercede, to approach on someone's behalf) — Abraham asks the assembly to approach Ephron as his intermediaries.
9: מְעָרַת הַמַּכְפֵּלָה אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ אֲשֶׁר בִּקְצֵה שָׂדֵהוּ — "the cave of Machpelah which belongs to him, which is at the end of his field." Maḵpēlāh — from kāpal (to double, fold over); possibly "the double cave" — a cave with two chambers. בְּכֶסֶף מָלֵא (bĕḵeseḵ mālēʾ, for the full price) — Abraham insists on paying the full market value; he will not receive the burial ground as a gift (which would leave a claim relationship on the property) or at a reduced price. Full payment = full legal title without ongoing obligation. The cave at Machpelah becomes the family tomb: Sarah (23:19), Abraham (25:9), Isaac and Rebekah, and Jacob and Leah (49:29-32; 50:13) will all be buried there.
10: וְעֶפְרוֹן יֹשֵׁב בְּתוֹךְ בְּנֵי-חֵת — "Ephron was sitting among the sons of Heth." Ephron was present in the assembly from the beginning — the narrative had been describing the community's corporate response; now the specific property owner is identified. The gate (šaʿar) is the commercial and judicial center of the ancient city: contracts, legal cases, marriages, and business were conducted "at the gate" (Ruth 4:1-12; Deut 21:19; Prov 31:23; Amos 5:15). Ephron responds publicly, in the hearing of all — the transaction is legally witnessed from the start.
11: לֹא-אֲדֹנִי שְׁמָעֵנִי הַשָּׂדֶה נָתַתִּי לָךְ — "No, my lord, hear me: I give you the field." Ephron's initial offer is the field plus the cave as a gift — three times he uses nātatî lĕḵā (I give to you): "I give you the field," "I give you the cave," "I give it to you in the presence of my people." The triple offer of a free gift is a formal ANE negotiating posture — generosity is offered publicly; the expected response is that the buyer will insist on paying. A gift-acceptance would create dependency and ongoing obligation; a purchase creates clean ownership.
12: וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ אַבְרָהָם לִפְנֵי עַם הָאָרֶץ — "Abraham bowed before the people of the land." The second bow acknowledges Ephron's offer and the community's witnessing role. The repetition of bowing (vv. 7 and 12) frames Abraham's second speech; each bow marks a stage of negotiation. The public assembly functions as the legal court witnessing every step of the transaction.
13: אַךְ אִם-אַתָּה לוּ שְׁמָעֵנִי נָתַתִּי כֶּסֶף הַשָּׂדֶה קַח מִמֶּנִּי — "but please, if you will hear me — I will give the price of the field; accept it from me." Abraham insists on paying: keseḵ hassāḏeh (the silver/price of the field) — he will pay the commercial value. Qah mimmennî (accept from me) — the polite imperative requesting that Ephron receive payment. By insisting on payment, Abraham converts the gift-offer into a purchase, securing clean legal title without ongoing obligation to the Hittite community.
14: The transition verse — Ephron's reply names the price in v. 15. The formal structure of the negotiation (offer → counter → price-naming → payment) follows attested ANE patterns for real-estate transactions. The speech formula wayyaʿan ʾeprôn ʾet-ʾaḇrāhām (Ephron answered Abraham) marks the decisive turn in the negotiation: the price will now be stated publicly, before witnesses.
15: אַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שֶׁקֶל-כֶּסֶף בֵּינִי וּבֵינְךָ מַה-הִוא — "four hundred shekels of silver — what is that between you and me?" The price named publicly is 400 shekels of silver — a high price for a field and cave. Comparable ANE property prices suggest 400 shekels is a premium price (Jeremiah purchases a field for 17 shekels, Jer 32:9; David purchases the threshing floor for 50 shekels, 2 Sam 24:24). Ephron's "what is that between you and me?" dismisses the price as negligible — a social fiction that simultaneously names the price and signals willingness to close the transaction.
16: וַיִּשְׁקֹל אַבְרָהָם לְעֶפְרֹן אֶת-הַכֶּסֶף (wayyišqōl ʾaḇrāhām lĕʿeprōn ʾet-hakkeseḵ) — "Abraham weighed out to Ephron the silver." Šāqal (to weigh) — the verb from which šeqel (shekel) is derived; the shekel was originally a weight standard, not a minted coin; silver was weighed on a balance scale in transactions. עֹבֵר לַסֹּחֵר (ʿōḇēr lassōḥēr, current among merchants) — silver at the weight-standard that passed/was accepted in commercial transactions; the commercial quality and purity specification ensures that both parties received equivalent value by recognized standards.
17: The deed-of-sale is recorded with legal precision. הַשָּׂדֶה וְהַמְּעָרָה אֲשֶׁר-בּוֹ וְכָל-הָעֵץ אֲשֶׁר בַּשָּׂדֶה — "the field and the cave in it and all the trees within the field" — three components: the field (land), the cave (specific structure), all trees within the borders (everything on the land). ANE property deeds commonly listed all components of the property, including trees, because trees represented independent value (for fruit, timber, shade). The precision mirrors actual ancient property deeds found at Nuzi and Ugarit.
18: לְאַבְרָהָם לְמִקְנָה לְעֵינֵי בְנֵי-חֵת — "to Abraham as a possession, in the eyes of the sons of Heth." Miqnāh (purchase, acquisition, something bought) — the key legal word: Abraham now owns the Machpelah by purchase, not by gift or divine covenant alone. בְּכֹל בָּאֵי שַׁעַר-עִירוֹ (before all who came in at the gate of his city) — the witnesses are "all who came in at the gate" — the entire civic assembly that regularly passes through the city gate is witness. Public transaction at the gate is the ANE equivalent of registered legal deed: the community is the witness.
19: וְאַחֲרֵי-כֵן קָבַר אַבְרָהָם אֶת-שָׂרָה אִשְׁתּוֹ — "afterward Abraham buried Sarah his wife." The legal transaction (vv. 3-18) precedes the burial — proper legal title secured before burial takes place. The burial location: bĕmĕʿārat śĕḏeh hamaḵpēlāh ʿal-pĕnê mamrēʾ hîʾ ḥeḇrôn bĕʾereṣ kĕnaʿan (in the cave of the field of Machpelah, before Mamre, which is Hebron, in the land of Canaan) — the full legal description of the burial site, exactly as in the deed (vv. 17-18). The repetition of the full description confirms that the burial fulfilled the terms of the purchase.
20: וַיָּקָם הַשָּׂדֶה וְהַמְּעָרָה אֲשֶׁר-בּוֹ לְאַבְרָהָם לַאֲחֻזַּת-קָבֶר מֵאֵת בְּנֵי-חֵת — "so the field and the cave in it were confirmed/transferred to Abraham as a burial possession from the sons of Heth." Wayyāqom (and it was confirmed/established, rose — Qal imperfect of qûm, to rise, stand, be confirmed; in legal contexts: to be established, confirmed as valid) — the deed is legally confirmed and standing. ʾAḥuzzat-qāḇer (burial possession/burial holding) — ʾăḥuzzāh (possession, holding, land title) is the legal term for territorial ownership used in the land-grant contexts (cf. Lev 25:10-13 for family land returns; Num 27:4; Josh 21:12). Abraham's one piece of legally owned land in all of Canaan is a burial cave — the covenant heir who was promised the whole land owns only a tomb.
